title: The compile-time horizon: a bright vertical bar divides definition time (left) from runtime (right). The left side shows the governing contract as colored bands — declarations in blue, transition rules in amber, the key governing rule in bright green. The right side is void except for a faint echo of the permitted rule. Precept's compiler draws this line: everything before it is the definition; everything after is only what the definition permits.
conceptualBridge: The compile-time horizon means Precept's verification is the boundary that separates definition from execution because the compiler's check draws an impenetrable line — nothing invalid can cross it into runtime, only what the contract explicitly declares.
decision: Rejected: 3.94 avg just below threshold — the compile-horizon concept is valid but the execution is too busy (7 bands + divider + echo); monochrome test is weak (3.5). A stronger iteration would reduce to 3 bands max and make the divider bolder relative to the bands.
